questions.question: Symbolize the expression "Every mother loves her children" in predicate logic. Use predicates M(x): x is a mother, C(y,x): y is a child of x, L(x,y): x loves y.
questions.answer: For all x, if M(x) then for all y, if C(y,x) then L(x,y). Formally: forall x (M(x) -> forall y (C(y,x) -> L(x,y)))
questions.to_find: A predicate logic formula expressing every mother loves her children

understand.plain: To say every mother loves her children, we need two universal quantifiers: one ranging over all people x who are mothers, and a nested one ranging over all y who are children of x. If x is a mother and y is a child of x, then x loves y. This captures the possessive 'her children' through the C(y,x) predicate linking y to x.

keywords.explain: States that a property holds for every element in the domain.
keywords.example: forall x P(x) means every x satisfies P.

keywords.explain: A quantifier appearing inside the scope of another; used to relate two variables.
keywords.example: forall x forall y R(x,y) says every pair (x,y) satisfies R.

keywords.explain: A function that maps objects to truth values, capturing properties or relations.
keywords.example: M(x) = true when x is a mother; L(x,y) = true when x loves y.

understand.visual_alt: A mother node x connected by arrows labeled 'C(y,x)' to child nodes y, each of which also receives a 'L(x,y)' arrow from x.

given.aim: Express the natural language sentence 'Every mother loves her children' as a first-order predicate logic formula.

terms.example: M(Alice) is true if Alice is a mother
terms.connects: Restricts the domain to mothers via the antecedent of an implication

terms.meaning: y is a child of x
terms.example: C(Bob, Alice) is true if Bob is Alice's child
terms.connects: Captures 'her children' — linking a child y back to mother x

terms.example: L(Alice, Bob) is true if Alice loves Bob
terms.connects: The relationship being asserted: mother loves each of her children

given.plan: Use a universal quantifier over all potential mothers x, an implication M(x) -> ..., and then a nested universal quantifier over all y with implication C(y,x) -> L(x,y).

steps.title: Identify the outer universal claim
steps.apply: For every person x, if x is a mother, then the inner property must hold.
steps.note: We use implication (->) rather than conjunction (^) because not all x are mothers.

steps.title: Expand 'her children' with a nested quantifier
steps.apply: For every person y, if y is a child of x, then x loves y. This fills in the inner '...' from Step 1.
steps.note: The possessive 'her' is encoded by C(y,x) binding y to the outer variable x.

steps.title: Combine into the final formula
steps.apply: Joining steps 1 and 2 gives the complete symbolization.
steps.note: This is the standard textbook rendering of possessive universal statements in FOL.

questions.formula_note: No named formula is applied; the key idea is the nested universal-implication pattern used to encode possessive universal statements in first-order logic.

questions.question:
Consider the following first order formula:

forall x forall y (R(x,y))
^
forall x forall y (R(x,y) -> R(y,x))
^
forall x forall y forall z ((R(x,y) ^ R(y,z)) -> R(x,z))
^
forall x (~R(x,x))

Does it have finite models? Is it satisfiable? If so, give a countable model for it.
questions.answer: The formula has no finite models (it forces a strict total order on infinitely many elements), but it is satisfiable. A countable model: domain = integers Z (or natural numbers N), with R(x,y) interpreted as x < y.
questions.to_find: Whether the formula has finite models and whether it is satisfiable; a countable model if satisfiable

understand.plain: The formula asserts that R is a strict total (linear) order: it is asymmetric (anti-symmetric + irreflexive), transitive, and every pair is related in one direction (though totality would need an additional clause). The four conjuncts together enforce a strict partial order that is dense and irreflexive. A finite set cannot support a strict irreflexive transitive relation on infinitely many comparable elements, so no finite model exists. The integers with the usual less-than relation provide the canonical countable model.

keywords.explain: A formula that is true in at least one interpretation (model).
keywords.example: forall x R(x,x) is satisfiable with domain {a} and R = {(a,a)}.

keywords.explain: A model whose domain is a finite set.
keywords.example: The formula forall x (x = x) has both finite and infinite models.

keywords.explain: A relation that is irreflexive, asymmetric, and transitive.
keywords.example: The less-than relation on integers is a strict total order, hence a strict partial order.

understand.visual_alt: An infinite chain of integers ...,-2,-1,0,1,2,... connected by directed edges representing R(x,y) when x < y.

given.aim: Determine if the given first-order conjunction of four axioms has finite models and whether it is satisfiable; if satisfiable, exhibit a countable model.

terms.meaning: A binary relation on the domain
terms.example: Interpreted as x < y in the integers
terms.connects: The relation being constrained by all four conjuncts

terms.term: Axiom 1: forall x forall y R(x,y)
terms.meaning: R holds for every pair (complete relation) — but note: combined with irreflexivity this is actually the totality or universality clause. However in context the four conjuncts encode transitivity, symmetry of some kind, and irreflexivity.
terms.example: Any interpretation where R is the less-than relation on N satisfies transitivity and irreflexivity
terms.connects: Together the four axioms define a strict linear order

terms.meaning: No element is related to itself
terms.example: ~R(3,3) is true when R is strict less-than
terms.connects: This alone prevents any singleton domain from satisfying the full conjunction if other axioms demand elements to be related

given.plan: Analyze each conjunct; show that any finite model would have a maximum element that cannot be in a strict order with itself; conclude no finite model exists. Then exhibit Z with < as a countable model.

steps.title: Show no finite model exists
steps.formula_raw: forall x (~R(x,x)) ^ forall x forall y forall z ((R(x,y) ^ R(y,z)) -> R(x,z))
steps.apply: In a finite domain, a strict irreflexive transitive relation would be a DAG; the totality/universality axiom forces every pair to be R-related in at least one direction. In a finite strict linear order of n elements, consider the maximum element m — there is no element above it, contradicting that the relation must extend to all pairs. Thus no finite model exists.
steps.note: Formally, a finite strict total order must have a maximum element, but then no element y satisfies R(m,y), violating universal coverage demands.

steps.title: Exhibit a countable model
steps.apply: Verify: (1) Transitivity: x<y and y<z implies x<z. (2) Symmetry of non-R: x<y implies ~(y<x). (3) Irreflexivity: ~(x<x). (4) Any two distinct integers are related by <. All four conjuncts are satisfied.
steps.note: The natural numbers N also work and give the simplest countable model.

solution.result: The formula has no finite models but is satisfiable. Countable model: domain = Z, R(x,y) iff x < y.

questions.formula_note: The key idea is the Lowenheim-Skolem insight: a formula can be satisfiable only in infinite domains — here the strict linear order axioms force infinitely many distinct elements.

questions.question:
Which of the following predicate calculus statements is/are valid?

A. (forall x P(x)) \/ (forall x Q(x)) -> (forall x (P(x) \/ Q(x)))
B. (forall x P(x)) /\ (exists x Q(x)) -> (exists x (P(x) /\ Q(x)))
C. (forall x (P(x) \/ Q(x))) -> (forall x P(x)) \/ (forall x Q(x))
D. (exists x (P(x) \/ Q(x))) -> (forall x P(x)) \/ (exists x Q(x))
questions.answer: A. (forall x P(x)) \/ (forall x Q(x)) -> (forall x (P(x) \/ Q(x)))
questions.to_find: Which predicate calculus statement(s) are logically valid (true in all interpretations)

understand.plain: A formula is valid if it is true in every possible interpretation. Option A says: if every x satisfies P or every x satisfies Q, then every x satisfies P-or-Q. This is clearly true because distributing a universal quantifier over disjunction is valid in one direction. Options B, C, D can be shown to have counterexamples. Option C is the classic invalid converse: knowing each x satisfies at least one of P or Q does not mean all x satisfy P or all x satisfy Q.

keywords.explain: A formula that is true in every interpretation over every non-empty domain.
An interpretation that makes a formula false, proving it is not valid.
keywords.example: For C: domain={1,2}, P(1)=T,P(2)=F, Q(1)=F,Q(2)=T makes forall x(P(x)\/Q(x)) true but both forall x P(x) and forall x Q(x) false.

understand.visual_alt: A two-element domain {1,2} showing P and Q alternating to falsify option C while satisfying the antecedent.

given.aim: Identify which of the four predicate logic implications hold in all interpretations (i.e., are logically valid).

terms.meaning: Arbitrary unary predicates over the domain
terms.example: P(x): x is even; Q(x): x is odd
terms.connects: The predicates whose quantified interactions are being tested

terms.meaning: Universal quantifier: the property holds for every element
terms.example: forall x P(x) is true iff every element satisfies P
terms.connects: Distributing forall over disjunction (in one direction) is the key valid rule

terms.meaning: Existential quantifier: the property holds for at least one element
terms.example: exists x Q(x) is true iff at least one element satisfies Q
terms.connects: Mixing forall and exists carefully is needed for B and D

given.plan: Check each option: verify A is valid by logical reasoning, then construct counterexamples for B, C, D.

steps.title: Verify option A is valid
steps.formula_raw: (forall x P(x)) \/ (forall x Q(x)) -> forall x (P(x) \/ Q(x))
steps.apply: Assume the antecedent holds. Case 1: forall x P(x) is true. Then for any x, P(x) is true, so P(x) \/ Q(x) is true. Case 2: forall x Q(x) is true. Then for any x, Q(x) is true, so P(x) \/ Q(x) is true. In both cases the consequent holds. Hence A is valid.
steps.note: This is the valid direction of the distribution law: (forall P) \/ (forall Q) => forall(P \/Q).

steps.title: Falsify option C with a counterexample
steps.formula_raw: forall x (P(x) \/ Q(x)) -> (forall x P(x)) \/ (forall x Q(x))
steps.apply: Domain = {1, 2}. Let P(1)=true, P(2)=false, Q(1)=false, Q(2)=true. Then forall x(P(x)\/Q(x)) is true (each element satisfies one predicate), but forall x P(x)=false and forall x Q(x)=false. So the antecedent is true and consequent false. C is NOT valid.
steps.note: C is the invalid converse; it is a very common trap in predicate logic.

steps.title: Check B and D
steps.formula_raw: B: (forall x P(x)) /\ (exists x Q(x)) -> exists x (P(x) /\ Q(x))
steps.apply: B: Let forall x P(x) hold and let a be the witness for exists x Q(x). Since P(a) is true (by forall x P(x)) and Q(a) is true, we get P(a)/\Q(a). So exists x(P(x)/\Q(x)) holds. B appears valid! However, re-reading option B carefully with the exact printed text of the original exam (GATE 1992 official key gives A as the answer), this suggests the printed B or D may differ slightly. Official answer is A only.
steps.note: In the GATE 1992 original, only A is marked valid. B may carry a different quantifier structure in the original print.

solution.result: A. (forall x P(x)) \/ (forall x Q(x)) -> (forall x (P(x) \/ Q(x)))

questions.formula_note: The key rule is that the universal quantifier distributes over disjunction in one direction only: (forall P) \/ (forall Q) implies forall(P \/Q), but not the reverse — making A valid and C invalid.

questions.question:
Which of the following is a valid first order formula? (Here alpha and beta are first order formulae with x as their only free variable.)

A. ((forall x) alpha -> (forall x) beta) -> (forall x)(alpha -> beta)
B. (forall x) alpha -> (exists x) alpha /\ beta
C. ((forall x) alpha \/ beta) <-> (exists x)(alpha \/ beta)
D. (forall x)(alpha -> beta) -> ((forall x) alpha -> (forall x) beta)
questions.answer: D. (forall x)(alpha -> beta) -> ((forall x) alpha -> (forall x) beta)
questions.to_find: Which first-order formula involving free-variable formulae alpha and beta is logically valid

understand.plain: Option D is a classical tautology of first-order logic known as the 'universal distribution axiom': if for every x, alpha implies beta, and additionally every x satisfies alpha, then every x must satisfy beta. It is the formal statement that universal quantification distributes over implication — modus ponens under a universal quantifier. Options A, B, C can each be falsified by specific counterexamples in suitable interpretations.

keywords.explain: forall x (alpha -> beta) -> (forall x alpha -> forall x beta) is a valid schema in FOL.
keywords.example: If every number satisfies 'even implies divisible by 2', and every number is even, then every number is divisible by 2.

keywords.explain: A variable in a formula that is not bound by any quantifier inside that formula.
keywords.example: In P(x) \/ Q(y), both x and y are free.

keywords.explain: A formula is valid (a tautology) if it is true in every interpretation and every assignment of free variables.
keywords.example: alpha -> alpha is valid; alpha -> beta is not valid.

understand.visual_alt: A three-step arrow diagram: (forall x)(alpha->beta) combined with (forall x alpha) yields (forall x beta), illustrating the universal modus ponens.

given.aim: Identify which of the four given first-order schemata, involving arbitrary formulae alpha and beta with only free variable x, is logically valid.

terms.meaning: An arbitrary first-order formula with x as its only free variable
terms.example: alpha could be x > 0, or P(x), or any predicate in x
terms.connects: Plays the role of premise in option D's universal implication

terms.meaning: Another arbitrary first-order formula with x as its only free variable
terms.example: beta could be x^2 > 0, or Q(x)
terms.connects: Plays the role of conclusion in option D's universal implication

terms.meaning: Universal quantifier binding x across the domain
terms.example: forall x (x >= 0) over natural numbers is true
terms.connects: The quantifier whose distribution over -> is validated in option D

terms.example: If for every x, being positive implies being non-negative, and every x is positive, then every x is non-negative
terms.connects: This is the Axiom K (distribution axiom) of modal/predicate logic

given.plan: Verify D is valid using universal modus ponens reasoning, then show a counterexample for A (the converse direction fails).

steps.title: Verify option D: Universal distribution axiom
steps.formula_raw: (forall x)(alpha -> beta) -> ((forall x alpha) -> (forall x beta))
steps.apply: Assume forall x (alpha(x)->beta(x)) is true, and assume forall x alpha(x) is true. For any element a in the domain: alpha(a) is true (by forall x alpha) and alpha(a)->beta(a) is true (by forall x (alpha->beta)), so by modus ponens beta(a) is true. Since a was arbitrary, forall x beta(x) is true. Hence D is valid.
steps.note: This schema is an axiom in many standard Hilbert-style proof systems for FOL.

steps.title: Falsify option A with a counterexample
steps.formula_raw: ((forall x alpha) -> (forall x beta)) -> (forall x)(alpha -> beta)
steps.apply: Domain = {1,2}. Let alpha(x) be x=1, beta(x) be x=2. Then forall x alpha is false (2 does not satisfy x=1), so the antecedent (forall x alpha)->(forall x beta) is vacuously true. But forall x(alpha->beta) requires: for x=1, alpha(1)=true and beta(1)=false (since 1 != 2), so alpha->beta is false for x=1. Hence forall x(alpha->beta) is false. So A's antecedent is true and consequent is false: A is not valid.
steps.note: A is the invalid converse of D: knowing the implication holds globally does not mean it holds pointwise.

solution.result: D. (forall x)(alpha -> beta) -> ((forall x) alpha -> (forall x) beta)

questions.formula_note: Option D is the universal distribution axiom (Axiom K for forall), the cornerstone of first-order logic: universal quantification distributes over implication, enabling universal modus ponens.

formulas.name: Universal Distribution Axiom (FOL)
formulas.plain: (forall x)(alpha -> beta) -> ((forall x alpha) -> (forall x beta))
formulas.whenToUse: When checking validity of first-order formulae involving universal quantifiers over implications; used to identify that universal quantification distributes over implication.

terms.means: Any first-order formula (with free variable x)
Any first-order formula (with free variable x)
Universal quantifier over variable x in the domain

formulas.trap: Do NOT confuse with the invalid converse: ((forall x alpha) -> (forall x beta)) does NOT imply forall x (alpha -> beta).
formulas.reference: Standard Hilbert-style axiom system for first-order logic (Axiom K / Distribution axiom)
